title: What is a Common Table Expression (CTE)?
description: What does WITH ... AS do in MySQL?
options: Defines a temporary named result set that can be referenced within a SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement
correct_answer: Defines a temporary named result set that can be referenced within a SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE statement

title: What is a recursive CTE?
description: How do recursive CTEs work?
options: A CTE that references itself with a base case (anchor) and recursive part, useful for hierarchical data
correct_answer: A CTE that references itself with a base case (anchor) and recursive part, useful for hierarchical data

title: What is ROW_NUMBER()?
description: What does the ROW_NUMBER() window function do?
options: Assigns a unique sequential integer to each row within a partition
correct_answer: Assigns a unique sequential integer to each row within a partition

title: What is RANK()?
description: How does RANK() differ from ROW_NUMBER()?
options: RANK() assigns the same rank to rows with equal values and leaves gaps; ROW_NUMBER() is always unique
correct_answer: RANK() assigns the same rank to rows with equal values and leaves gaps; ROW_NUMBER() is always unique

title: What is DENSE_RANK()?
description: How does DENSE_RANK() differ from RANK()?
options: DENSE_RANK() assigns consecutive ranks without gaps for tied values; RANK() leaves gaps
correct_answer: DENSE_RANK() assigns consecutive ranks without gaps for tied values; RANK() leaves gaps

title: What is NTILE()?
description: What does NTILE(n) do?
options: Divides an ordered partition into n roughly equal groups and assigns a group number to each row
correct_answer: Divides an ordered partition into n roughly equal groups and assigns a group number to each row

title: What is LAG()?
description: What does the LAG() window function do?
options: Accesses a value from a previous row within the same partition without a self-join
correct_answer: Accesses a value from a previous row within the same partition without a self-join

title: What is LEAD()?
description: What does the LEAD() window function do?
options: Accesses a value from a subsequent row within the same partition without a self-join
correct_answer: Accesses a value from a subsequent row within the same partition without a self-join

title: What is EXPLAIN in MySQL?
description: What does EXPLAIN show?
options: Shows the execution plan for a query, including how tables are accessed, join types, and index usage
correct_answer: Shows the execution plan for a query, including how tables are accessed, join types, and index usage

title: What is the difference between WHERE and HAVING?
description: How do WHERE and HAVING differ?
options: WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY aggregation
correct_answer: WHERE filters rows before grouping; HAVING filters groups after GROUP BY aggregation

title: What is GROUP_CONCAT()?
description: What does GROUP_CONCAT() do?
options: Concatenates values from multiple rows into a single comma-separated string within each group
correct_answer: Concatenates values from multiple rows into a single comma-separated string within each group

title: What is the COALESCE function?
description: What does COALESCE() do?
options: Returns the first non-NULL value from a list of arguments
correct_answer: Returns the first non-NULL value from a list of arguments

title: What is IFNULL()?
description: What does IFNULL() do?
options: Returns the first argument if it's not NULL; otherwise returns the second argument
correct_answer: Returns the first argument if it's not NULL; otherwise returns the second argument

title: What is the CASE expression?
description: How does CASE work in MySQL?
options: A conditional expression that returns different values based on conditions (like if-else)
correct_answer: A conditional expression that returns different values based on conditions (like if-else)

title: What is a covering index?
description: What makes an index a covering index?
options: An index that contains all columns needed by a query, so MySQL can return results from the index alone without reading table data
correct_answer: An index that contains all columns needed by a query, so MySQL can return results from the index alone without reading table data

title: What is the difference between CHAR and VARCHAR?
description: How do CHAR and VARCHAR differ?
options: CHAR is fixed-length (padded with spaces); VARCHAR is variable-length (stores actual length + length prefix)
correct_answer: CHAR is fixed-length (padded with spaces); VARCHAR is variable-length (stores actual length + length prefix)

title: What is the difference between DATETIME and TIMESTAMP?
description: How do they differ?
options: TIMESTAMP is UTC-based and auto-converts to timezone (4 bytes, range 1970-2038); DATETIME stores as-is (8 bytes, wider range)
correct_answer: TIMESTAMP is UTC-based and auto-converts to timezone (4 bytes, range 1970-2038); DATETIME stores as-is (8 bytes, wider range)

title: What is the ENUM type?
description: What does ENUM do in MySQL?
options: A string column that can only contain one value from a predefined list, stored internally as integers
correct_answer: A string column that can only contain one value from a predefined list, stored internally as integers

title: What is a composite index?
description: What is a composite (multi-column) index?
options: An index on multiple columns; queries benefit from it only when using a leftmost prefix of the indexed columns
correct_answer: An index on multiple columns; queries benefit from it only when using a leftmost prefix of the indexed columns

title: What is the leftmost prefix rule?
description: How does the leftmost prefix rule affect composite indexes?
options: MySQL can only use a composite index if the query uses columns starting from the leftmost column of the index in order
The rightmost column is checked first
correct_answer: MySQL can only use a composite index if the query uses columns starting from the leftmost column of the index in order

title: What is EXPLAIN ANALYZE?
description: What does EXPLAIN ANALYZE provide over EXPLAIN?
options: Actually executes the query and shows both estimated and actual execution times, rows, and iterations
correct_answer: Actually executes the query and shows both estimated and actual execution times, rows, and iterations

title: What is the slow query log?
description: What does the slow query log capture?
options: Logs queries that take longer than a specified threshold (long_query_time) for performance analysis
correct_answer: Logs queries that take longer than a specified threshold (long_query_time) for performance analysis

title: What is a deadlock?
description: What causes a deadlock in MySQL?
options: Two or more transactions waiting for each other to release locks, creating a circular dependency; InnoDB detects and rolls back one
correct_answer: Two or more transactions waiting for each other to release locks, creating a circular dependency; InnoDB detects and rolls back one

title: What are the ACID properties?
description: What does ACID stand for?
options: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — properties that guarantee reliable transactions
correct_answer: Atomicity, Consistency, Isolation, Durability — properties that guarantee reliable transactions

title: What are transaction isolation levels?
description: Name the four SQL isolation levels.
options: READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE
correct_answer: READ UNCOMMITTED, READ COMMITTED, REPEATABLE READ, SERIALIZABLE

title: What is REPEATABLE READ?
description: What does REPEATABLE READ guarantee?
options: Ensures that if a transaction reads data twice, it sees the same data (no non-repeatable reads); InnoDB's default level
correct_answer: Ensures that if a transaction reads data twice, it sees the same data (no non-repeatable reads); InnoDB's default level

title: What is a phantom read?
description: What is a phantom read in databases?
options: When a transaction re-executes a range query and gets different rows because another transaction inserted/deleted rows
correct_answer: When a transaction re-executes a range query and gets different rows because another transaction inserted/deleted rows

title: What is the difference between DELETE, TRUNCATE, and DROP?
description: How do they differ?
options: DELETE removes rows (logged, WHERE clause, rollback possible); TRUNCATE removes all rows (faster, no WHERE); DROP removes the entire table
correct_answer: DELETE removes rows (logged, WHERE clause, rollback possible); TRUNCATE removes all rows (faster, no WHERE); DROP removes the entire table

title: What is a foreign key?
description: What does a foreign key enforce?
options: Referential integrity between two tables: the value must exist in the referenced (parent) table's referenced column
correct_answer: Referential integrity between two tables: the value must exist in the referenced (parent) table's referenced column

title: What is ON DELETE CASCADE?
description: What does ON DELETE CASCADE do?
options: Automatically deletes child rows when the referenced parent row is deleted
correct_answer: Automatically deletes child rows when the referenced parent row is deleted

title: What is a subquery?
description: What is a subquery?
options: A query nested inside another query, used in WHERE, FROM, SELECT, or HAVING clauses
correct_answer: A query nested inside another query, used in WHERE, FROM, SELECT, or HAVING clauses

title: What is a correlated subquery?
description: How does a correlated subquery differ from a regular subquery?
options: A correlated subquery references the outer query and is executed once for each row of the outer query
correct_answer: A correlated subquery references the outer query and is executed once for each row of the outer query

title: What is the EXISTS clause?
description: How does EXISTS work?
options: Returns TRUE if the subquery returns any rows; often more efficient than IN for correlated subqueries
correct_answer: Returns TRUE if the subquery returns any rows; often more efficient than IN for correlated subqueries

title: What is the UNION operator?
description: What does UNION do?
options: Combines results of two SELECT statements, removing duplicates (UNION ALL keeps duplicates)
correct_answer: Combines results of two SELECT statements, removing duplicates (UNION ALL keeps duplicates)

title: What is a materialized view?
description: Does MySQL support materialized views?
options: MySQL doesn't natively support materialized views; they can be simulated with tables + triggers/events
correct_answer: MySQL doesn't natively support materialized views; they can be simulated with tables + triggers/events

title: What is the difference between INNER JOIN and CROSS JOIN?
description: How do they differ?
options: INNER JOIN returns rows with matching values in both tables; CROSS JOIN returns the Cartesian product of all rows
correct_answer: INNER JOIN returns rows with matching values in both tables; CROSS JOIN returns the Cartesian product of all rows

title: What is a NATURAL JOIN?
description: How does NATURAL JOIN work?
options: Automatically joins tables on columns with the same name and compatible types, without specifying ON
correct_answer: Automatically joins tables on columns with the same name and compatible types, without specifying ON

title: What is the REPLACE statement?
description: What does REPLACE INTO do?
options: Inserts a row, or if a duplicate key exists, deletes the old row and inserts the new one
correct_answer: Inserts a row, or if a duplicate key exists, deletes the old row and inserts the new one

title: What is INSERT ... ON DUPLICATE KEY UPDATE?
description: What does this statement do?
options: Inserts a row; if a duplicate key conflict occurs, updates the existing row instead
correct_answer: Inserts a row; if a duplicate key conflict occurs, updates the existing row instead

title: What is the JSON data type?
description: What does the JSON column type provide in MySQL?
options: Native JSON storage with validation and efficient access via functions like JSON_EXTRACT, ->>, JSON_SET
correct_answer: Native JSON storage with validation and efficient access via functions like JSON_EXTRACT, ->>, JSON_SET

title: What is a generated column?
description: What are generated (virtual/stored) columns?
options: Columns whose values are computed from an expression on other columns; VIRTUAL is computed on read, STORED is persisted
correct_answer: Columns whose values are computed from an expression on other columns; VIRTUAL is computed on read, STORED is persisted

title: What is the InnoDB buffer pool?
description: What does the buffer pool do?
options: Caches table data and indexes in memory to reduce disk I/O; the most critical InnoDB performance setting
correct_answer: Caches table data and indexes in memory to reduce disk I/O; the most critical InnoDB performance setting

title: What is the query cache?
description: What happened to the MySQL query cache?
options: Removed in MySQL 8.0 because it caused contention issues; was a cache of SELECT results keyed by query string
correct_answer: Removed in MySQL 8.0 because it caused contention issues; was a cache of SELECT results keyed by query string

title: What is table partitioning?
description: What does partitioning do?
options: Splits a large table into smaller physical pieces (partitions) while appearing as one logical table, improving query performance on specific ranges
correct_answer: Splits a large table into smaller physical pieces (partitions) while appearing as one logical table, improving query performance on specific ranges

title: Write a query to find nth highest salary.
description: Write a MySQL query to find the nth highest distinct salary from an employees table.

title: Write a query for running total.
description: Write a query to compute a running total of sales ordered by date.
correct_answer:
SELECT date, amount,
  SUM(amount) OVER (ORDER BY date) AS running_total
FROM sales
ORDER BY date;

title: Write a query to find employees earning more than their manager.
description: Given an employees table with id, name, salary, and manager_id, find employees who earn more than their manager.
correct_answer:
SELECT e.name AS employee, e.salary AS emp_salary, m.name AS manager, m.salary AS mgr_salary
FROM employees e
JOIN employees m ON e.manager_id = m.id
WHERE e.salary > m.salary;

title: Write a query for year-over-year growth.
description: Calculate the percentage growth in total revenue compared to the previous year.
correct_answer:
WITH yearly AS (
  SELECT YEAR(order_date) AS yr, SUM(amount) AS total
  FROM orders GROUP BY yr
)
SELECT yr, total,
  LAG(total) OVER (ORDER BY yr) AS prev_total,
  ROUND((total - LAG(total) OVER (ORDER BY yr)) / LAG(total) OVER (ORDER BY yr) * 100, 2) AS growth_pct
FROM yearly;

title: Write a query to find consecutive login days.
description: Find users who logged in for at least 3 consecutive days, given a logins(user_id, login_date) table.
correct_answer:
WITH numbered AS (
  SELECT user_id, login_date,
    ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY user_id ORDER BY login_date) AS rn
  FROM (SELECT DISTINCT user_id, login_date FROM logins) t
),
grouped AS (
  SELECT user_id, login_date,
    DATE_SUB(login_date, INTERVAL rn DAY) AS grp
  FROM numbered
)
SELECT user_id, MIN(login_date) AS start_date, MAX(login_date) AS end_date,
  COUNT(*) AS consecutive_days
FROM grouped
GROUP BY user_id, grp
HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3;

title: What is the difference between IN and EXISTS?
description: When should you use EXISTS over IN?
options: EXISTS is better for correlated subqueries and large subquery results; IN is better for small value lists and uncorrelated subqueries
correct_answer: EXISTS is better for correlated subqueries and large subquery results; IN is better for small value lists and uncorrelated subqueries

title: What is a clustered index?
description: How does a clustered index work in InnoDB?
options: In InnoDB, the primary key IS the clustered index; data rows are stored in the leaf pages of the B+tree ordered by the primary key
correct_answer: In InnoDB, the primary key IS the clustered index; data rows are stored in the leaf pages of the B+tree ordered by the primary key

title: What is index selectivity?
description: What does index selectivity measure?
options: The ratio of distinct values to total rows; higher selectivity (closer to 1) means the index is more effective
correct_answer: The ratio of distinct values to total rows; higher selectivity (closer to 1) means the index is more effective

title: What is the difference between MyISAM and InnoDB?
description: How do they differ?
options: InnoDB supports transactions, row-level locking, foreign keys, and crash recovery; MyISAM has table-level locking, no transactions, but faster full-text search (historically)
correct_answer: InnoDB supports transactions, row-level locking, foreign keys, and crash recovery; MyISAM has table-level locking, no transactions, but faster full-text search (historically)

title: What is a stored procedure?
description: What is a stored procedure?
options: A precompiled set of SQL statements stored in the database that can be executed by name with parameters
correct_answer: A precompiled set of SQL statements stored in the database that can be executed by name with parameters

title: What is a trigger?
description: What is a database trigger?
options: Code that automatically executes in response to INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE events on a table
correct_answer: Code that automatically executes in response to INSERT, UPDATE, or DELETE events on a table

title: What is an event scheduler?
description: What does the MySQL event scheduler do?
options: Executes scheduled tasks (SQL statements) at specified intervals or times, like a database cron job
correct_answer: Executes scheduled tasks (SQL statements) at specified intervals or times, like a database cron job

title: What is DISTINCT?
description: What does SELECT DISTINCT do?
options: Returns only unique rows, removing duplicates from the result set
correct_answer: Returns only unique rows, removing duplicates from the result set

title: What is LIMIT and OFFSET?
description: What do LIMIT and OFFSET do?
options: LIMIT restricts the number of rows returned; OFFSET skips a specified number of rows (used for pagination)
correct_answer: LIMIT restricts the number of rows returned; OFFSET skips a specified number of rows (used for pagination)

title: What is the BETWEEN operator?
description: What does BETWEEN do?
options: Selects values within a range (inclusive of both endpoints)
correct_answer: Selects values within a range (inclusive of both endpoints)

title: What is the LIKE operator?
description: How does LIKE work with wildcards?
options: Pattern matching with % (any number of chars) and _ (single char)
correct_answer: Pattern matching with % (any number of chars) and _ (single char)

title: What is the IN operator?
description: What does WHERE column IN (...) do?
options: Matches if the column value equals any value in the specified list
correct_answer: Matches if the column value equals any value in the specified list
